FT_IC1.evidence: Participants were 9254 youth from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) measured with non-educational activities: TV shows/movies, videos, video games on computer/console/phone, messaging on phone, and social networking sites

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic mixed models with chi-square and ANOVA tests linking SMA to recreational activity endorsement outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: OR values reported: games/movies vs social media OR=0.83 (95% CI 0.76-0.89); general SMA with soccer OR=0.93 (0.88-0.98); general SMA with baseball OR=0.92 (0.86-0.98)

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis using ABCD baseline data

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in BMC Public Health 2020

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports cross-sectional analyses with SMA as independent variable predicting recreational activity outcomes; FT-EC7 does not apply
notes: Recreation displacement hypothesis tested; paper finds minimal displacement effects

FT_IC1.evidence: Study utilized baseline data from Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study with 9986 families

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time types measured: TV/movie, video, video game, texting, social media, video chatting, and mature video games/R-rated movies; age-inappropriate screen time content specifically analyzed

FT_IC3.evidence: Network graphical models with statistical tests; permutation-based network comparison tests examining screen time associations with mood symptoms

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple test statistics and p-values reported; network analysis tests with significance indicators (e.g., age-inappropriate screen time stably significantly associated with mood symptoms, p<0.05)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical network analysis using mixed graphical models

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Journal of Abnormal Child Psychology 2020

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological/behavioral predictors (BIS/BAS systems, parental monitoring); reports screen time associations with psychological outcomes (mood symptoms)
notes: Individual psychological characteristics (BIS/BAS) treated as predictors; screen time tested as predictor of mood symptom outcomes

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional study uses Dutch ABCD Study (children 9-11 years, n=2,952) and Spanish INMA Project (n=261); NOT U.S. ABCD Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured via information on children's use of telecommunication and screen devices: mobile phones, tablets, and laptops

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple regression models testing associations between RF-EMF doses, screen time, and cognitive function outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: Beta coefficients reported: RF-EMF beta -0.01 points (95%CI -0.02; -0.01) for non-verbal IQ; beta 970.07 milliseconds (95%CI 60.28; 1879.85) for visuomotor coordination

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis with cognitive testing outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC1 (not U.S. ABCD); FT_EC7 not applicable
notes: EXCLUDED at FT_IC1 level - uses Dutch ABCD and Spanish INMA Project, not U.S. ABCD Study
